title: Scientists Find Old Life in a Surprising Place
summary: Scientists were hiking in Morocco when they saw something strange in rocks. They found tiny ridges called wrinkle structures, which are usually made by algae in shallow water where sunlight reaches. But these wrinkles were in deep water rocks, too dark for algae to live. This means they might have been made by different tiny life forms that don't need sunlight. The rocks are about 180 million years old, and such wrinkles are rare because animals often destroy them. The scientists tested the rocks and found clues that show these wrinkles came from living things. This discovery helps us learn about ancient life in the deep ocean.

background_read: Scientists study rocks to learn about Earth's history. Wrinkle structures are tiny patterns made by microbial mats, which are layers of tiny living things like algae or bacteria. In shallow water, algae use sunlight to make energy and create these wrinkles. In deep water, it's dark, so other microbes called chemosynthetic bacteria use chemicals instead of sunlight. Rocks can preserve these patterns if animals don't disturb them. This helps scientists understand how life existed in ancient oceans.
Article_Structure: WHO: Dr. Rowan Martindale and her team of scientists
WHAT: Found wrinkle structures in deep water rocks in Morocco
WHERE: In the Dadès Valley in Morocco's Central High Atlas Mountains
WHY: To learn about ancient life that didn't need sunlight

perspectives.description: Finding wrinkle structures in deep water shows that ancient life could thrive without sunlight, expanding our knowledge of where life can exist.
